title: Top 10 AI Workflow Frameworks for Data and Agents | Orchestra
text:
Preface
At Orchestra we’re focused on making data engineers’ lives easier by building an innovative consolidated orchestration and observability platform. The advantage of having a single control plane is that architecturally, you as a data team aren’t paying 50 different vendors for 50 different compute clusters, all of which cost time and money to maintain.
For too long, having a slick UI for rapidly building pipelines, end-to-end lineage, and visibility of exactly what’s going on in your data estate have been features of a data platform reserved only for large companies with big budgets. You can get started with Orchestra for free and can see it in Action here
Want to see how Orchestra is saving Data Teams hours building and maintaining Data Products? Check us out below.
Introduction
AI agents and data-driven applications are no longer isolated proof-of-concepts—they’re part of production-grade pipelines. Whether you're deploying retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) agents on Snowflake or chaining vector stores in Databricks, your AI workflows need structure, observability, and orchestration. Here are the top 10 frameworks powering real-world AI workflows in 2024:
1. LangChain
LangChain is the dominant open-source framework for building context-aware, agentic AI workflows. It supports chaining models, tools, memory, and retrieval, and integrates seamlessly with vector databases, APIs, and LLMs. LangChain’s abstraction layer lets you shift between local, OpenAI, or Anthropic backends without major refactoring.
2. Haystack
Built by deepset, Haystack specializes in production-ready RAG pipelines. It supports Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Weaviate, and integrates well with Hugging Face and OpenAI models. Ideal for enterprise search or FAQ agents, with solid observability and testing hooks.
3. Databricks MLflow + Mosaic AI
If you're building on Databricks, MLflow and Mosaic AI offer native support for model lifecycle management, prompt engineering, and fine-tuning. Mosaic’s Agent Framework builds on Spark-native components, with deep integration into Unity Catalog and Lakehouse assets.
4. Snowflake Cortex & Snowpark
Snowflake’s Cortex and Snowpark provide an end-to-end platform for running AI agents close to your data. You can call LLMs natively in SQL or Python, use native vector search, and orchestrate with external tools like dbt or Airflow. It’s a strong option for companies already invested in Snowflake’s data governance.
5. LlamaIndex (formerly GPT Index)
LlamaIndex acts as a flexible interface between your structured and unstructured data and your LLMs. It’s especially useful for indexing PDFs, SQL, or APIs into a retriever-aware format. You can pair it with LangChain or run standalone.
6. Orchestra
Orchestra is a YAML-native orchestration framework purpose-built for modern data and AI workflows. It supports step-level dependencies between ingestion (e.g., Fivetran), transformation (e.g., dbt), and inference (e.g., LangChain), ensuring agents only run once the data is reliable.
7. Airflow + LangChain Provider
Apache Airflow remains the backbone of data orchestration in many organizations. With the LangChain provider, you can now trigger agent runs, monitor tools, and schedule context refreshes directly in a DAG.
8. Transformers Agents by Hugging Face
This lightweight library lets you build multimodal agents using models from Hugging Face’s hub. It’s best for research or GPU-heavy workloads that involve images, text, or speech. You’ll need to handle persistence and tooling integration yourself.
9. PromptLayer
PromptLayer is a version control and observability layer for LLMs and agent workflows. It integrates with OpenAI and LangChain to log prompts, responses, and latencies—useful for debugging, A/B testing, or performance monitoring.
10. AutoGen by Microsoft
AutoGen is built for multi-agent conversations. It uses a “GroupChat” abstraction to coordinate multiple LLM agents working together. If your use case includes planning, reasoning, or tool-use delegation, AutoGen’s loop structure is worth exploring.
Final Thoughts
The right AI workflow framework depends on your existing stack, governance needs, and whether you’re running agents in production. Snowflake and Databricks users should evaluate native offerings like Cortex or Mosaic AI, while startups may prefer LangChain or LlamaIndex for speed and flexibility. Whichever you choose, orchestration is no longer optional—it’s the foundation for making AI useful, observable, and dependable.
Find out more about Orchestra
Orchestra is a low-code data orchestration and data observability platform. It’s also a build any DAG you can build in Airflow using orchestra. You can use it to solve many use-cases and solutions like swiftly building data products, preserving data quality, and even data governance. Our docs are here, but why not also check out our integrations - we manage these so you can get started with your pipelines instantly. We also have a blog, written by the Orchestra team + guest writers, and some whitepapers for more in-depth reads.
Which framework is best for AI agent?
The best framework for building an AI agent depends on your specific use case, but LangChain is currently the most widely adopted and flexible choice, especially for LLM-powered agents.
LangChain is ideal if you're building an agent that needs to reason, use tools (like web search or APIs), access memory, and interact in multi-step conversations. It’s well-documented, highly extensible, and integrates with popular LLMs like OpenAI, Anthropic, and open-source models. It also supports Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), making it great for agents that need to reference external knowledge or documents.
However, if your focus is on multi-agent collaboration, AutoGen by Microsoft may be better suited. For enterprise-grade copilots with tight integration into business applications, Semantic Kernel is a strong choice. And if you're working in information retrieval or question answering, LlamaIndex or Haystack will likely be more appropriate.
In short:
- Choose LangChain for general-purpose, tool-using LLM agents.
- Choose AutoGen for multi-agent reasoning.
- Choose Semantic Kernel for copilots with structured planning.
- Choose LlamaIndex or Haystack for document-heavy use cases.
What is an AI agent and how does it work?
An AI agent is a system that can perceive its environment, process information, and take actions to achieve a specific goal, often without human intervention. It works by following a continuous cycle: first, it gathers input through sensors or data sources—this could be text, images, audio, or structured data. Then, it processes this input using logic, rules, or trained machine learning models to make decisions. Finally, it acts based on that reasoning, which could mean sending a response, triggering a task, or making a prediction.
For example, in a chatbot, the AI agent receives a user question (input), understands it using natural language processing, decides on an appropriate answer (reasoning), and responds with a reply (action). In more advanced systems like autonomous vehicles or workflow automation tools, AI agents might combine multiple inputs, continuously learn from feedback, and interact with other agents or tools to complete more complex tasks. The agent’s ability to adapt, reason, and act in dynamic environments is what makes it intelligent.
